title: Photon-Atom Granularity Noise Thermometry
authors: Chen-Rong Liu, Yixuan Wang, Xiaowei Wang, Chuang Li, Mingti Zhou, Runxia Tao, Hongwei Chen, Ying Dong
abstract: We propose granularity noise thermometry (GNT), a fluctuation-based optical thermometry scheme that exploits the intrinsic fluctuations of susceptibility arising from atomic discreteness. The power spectral density of transmitted light exhibits an excess noise above the shot-noise limit that scales linearly with the photon-to-atom ratio $\mathcal{R}$. Consequently, varying the incident power (hence $\mathcal{R}$) yields the slope $\mathcal{K}$ of this linear scaling, which directly encodes the temperature. Closed-form expressions for the polarizability moments are derived via the plasma dispersion function, which yield distinct temperature scalings: $\mathcal{K}\propto P_{\mathrm{v}}(T)/T^2$ for thermal vapors and $\mathcal{K}\propto T^{2}$ for cold atoms. While practical implementation requires careful control of technical noise and system parameters, the present framework provides a noise-based pathway for optical thermometry using atomic ensembles.
summary: This paper proposes 'Granularity Noise Thermometry,' a method to measure temperature by analyzing the excess noise in light transmitted through atomic ensembles. It establishes that the noise scales linearly with the photon-to-atom ratio, allowing temperature extraction from the slope of this scaling. The technique is applicable to both warm atomic vapors and ultracold atomic clouds.
main_problem: The development of a new optical thermometry scheme, Granularity Noise Thermometry (GNT), to measure temperature by exploiting intrinsic fluctuations in atomic susceptibility, particularly in regimes where conventional Doppler-based methods fail.
main_result: The authors derive a unified scaling law where the excess noise in transmitted light power scales linearly with the photon-to-atom ratio, providing a temperature-dependent slope that works for both thermal vapors and ultracold atoms.
method: The study uses linear response theory, the plasma dispersion function for analytical moments, and a Fisher information framework to evaluate the precision of the proposed thermometry.
model_or_system: The system consists of two-level atomic ensembles (e.g., Cesium) in two regimes: thermal vapors and cold atom clouds, interacting with a coherent-state probe laser beam.
key_observables: Normalized Power Spectral Density (PSD) of transmitted light, photon-to-atom ratio (R), and the scaling slope (K).
important_parameters: Photon-to-atom ratio (R), Doppler broadening parameter (b0), saturation parameter (x), and the temperature-dependent scaling regimes (Doppler-dominated vs. homogeneous-broadening-dominated).
main_assumptions: Assumes a large number of atoms (Gaussian susceptibility), a coherent-state probe with Poissonian statistics, and that photon counts and atomic susceptibility are statistically independent.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the GNT experimental setup; Figure 2 illustrates the linear scaling of the PSD ratio with incident power; Figure 3 shows the transition of normalized variance between asymptotic limits; Figure 4 compares thermometric performance (sensitivity) across temperature ranges.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the GNT concept, establishes the physical model and scaling laws using statistical mechanics, derives analytical closed-form expressions for different temperature regimes, evaluates measurement precision via Fisher information, and concludes with a comparison of performance in vapor and cold atom systems.
why_interesting: It presents a novel way to use quantum/atomic noise as a signal rather than a nuisance, offering a high-precision sensing technique that remains functional even in the ultracold limit where traditional Doppler thermometry is unusable.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic of the GNT. A laser is amplitude-modulated (e.g., by an acousto-optic modulator) to vary the incident power 𝑃in and thereby the photon flux. The beam with waist 𝑤0 interacts with an atomic vapor cell of length 𝐿; for a cold atomic cloud, the physical cell is replaced by a trapped ensemble, and 𝐿is understood as the effective optical length 𝐿eﬀof the cloud. The transmitted power 𝑃tr is measured by a low-noise photodetector, and its power spectral density is recorded by a spectrum analyzer. The measured PSD ratio follows the scaling law of Eq. (7), where R denotes the photon‑to‑atom ratio (see text).

FIG. 2. PSD ratio 𝑆𝑃/𝑆(0) 𝑃 −1 as a function of incident power 𝑃in for several atomic vapor temperatures (173–473 K), computed using the cesium parameters from Table I. The shaded region indicates the weak‑probe regime where the saturation parameter 𝑥≪1 (see Ap- pendix B); the room‑temperature saturation power 𝑃sat ≈17 𝜇W is marked by the red arrow ▼.

Solid black curve: exact result from Eq. (17); red dashed line: 𝑏0 ≪1 approximation ∝1/𝑏0; blue dash-dotted line: 𝑏0 ≫1 asymptote ∝1/𝑏6 0. The inset shows 1/𝑏0 versus tem- perature 𝑇, with the black solid line including transit and collision broadening (Γ𝑡, Γ𝑐) and the gray dashed line corresponding to the ideal case Γ →0 (i.e., Γ𝑡= Γ𝑐= 0).

FIG. 4. Slope K as a function of temperature. Left: cold atoms (1 nK–1 K), with exact Kc (dark solid) and 𝑏0 ≫1 asymptote ∝𝑇2 (dashed with circular markers). Right: atomic vapor (150–650 K), with exact Kv (dark solid) and 𝑏0 ≪1 approximation ∝𝑃v(𝑇)/𝑇2 (dashed with diamond markers). The temperature axis is broken between 1 K and 150 K. Both axes use log-log scale. Parameters are listed in Table I.
